top_20.text:
My sentiment is this type of thing happens all the time. This is just a recent example that I think is clear. Btw the topic was about sparring in hip hop re: the Hov interview. 

This is not just about J Cole this is about lyrical rap as a subgenre period. My sentiment is that there’s a scarcity mindset in the subgenre and usually the top guys don’t invite other hungry spitters onto their own tracks. Let me give Cole his flowers real quick just to be clear because i loved the album and Ive always loved him as an emcee (and person). But I stand on what I’m saying. The evidence is there. There’s a difference between doing features for niggas and featuring niggas on your own work. And for the record, if niggas wholeheartedly thought JID didn’t fit on TFO, that’s your opinion. But he’s also not featured on any other j Cole album.

Didn’t J Cole just say “if hip hop was dead JID would be platinum?” (On a throwaway mixtape btw) But then not feature him at all on the mixtape or the album when either or would’ve made sense.

All I’m saying is notice the trend in this specific sub genre. 

- sincerely a lover of the art form of lyrical rap.
Where’s the underground hip hop Spotify https://t.co/PMOEKIUv0E

I've been consumed by Suno music the last few days. Finally took the plunge & signed up for a Pro account, making beginner mistakes & finding my way around the landscape.

Grok was both helpful & unhelpful. He's good at talking you thru instructions in various programs - how to do things, what the individual tools & buttons do. But lacks a contextual overview. He kept recommending that I open my piece in Suno's song editor, which I absolutely wasn't seeing. I finally thought to ask, "Is this a feature that exists only on desktop?" ... Sure enough, there it was in the desktop mode when I switched my view! If it had occurred to Grok that maybe this was the problem, & it's surely a common one, I might not have burned thru half my monthly allocated credits on 1 song!

Learning experience. As I listened to the different iterations, I found I would nod or make an approving gesture when something hit just right (that little flash of dopamine you get from your favorite part of a song!) - & I would wince or frown at mispronounced words or clunker notes. (Who knew that Suno can't pronounce "retrieve," no matter how phonetically you spell it?) And I thought, wouldn't it be nice if AI could read your body language & facial expressions in real time, & adjust on the fly? Not just for creating music, but for art, conversational feedback, & any number of other things. It would know organically when it got something wrong, or when you didn't quite understand & it needs to explain another way.

I think that's coming. I HOPE that's coming! As miraculous as AI collaboration seems to us now, it's going to look clunky & awkward in just a few years. Like the way we laugh at the giant brick of a cell phone in "Wall Street," which was the pinnacle of luxury at the time.

This is the future we're fighting for. Don't let deluded decelerationists stand in the way!

Growing up on a combination of reggae, underground hip hop, and pulp science fiction gives you a great appreciation for the new world religions which emerged in the west during the 20th century.  For example, the 5% Nation of Islam HEAVILY influenced hip hop culture in the 90s.  Same with Scientology emerging and influencing science fiction in the 70s.  

The cool thing about these religious movement is that they are fundamentally AMERICAN but resist easy categorization in our current political or religious discourse.  

Which is something I think the current Catholic and trad religion revival misses.  And presents a false dichotomy between either joining traditional old world religions like the Catholic Church or being a godless shitlib atheist.

Nettspend (left) is a 19-year-old rapper from Richmond, Virginia, who's blown up in the underground hip-hop scene with viral snippets, raw energy, and tracks like "drankdrankdrank." Nardwuar (right) is the legendary Canadian interviewer known for his plaid hat, wild questions, and "doot doot doo!" hype.

Nettspend (real name Gunner Shepardson) is a 19-year-old rapper from Richmond, Virginia, who's blown up in the underground hip-hop scene with his raw, viral tracks and internet fame. Nardwuar is the legendary Canadian interviewer (Nardwuar the Human Serviette) famous for his high-energy, super-researched chats with musicians that often go viral.
